Article.ArticleTitle: Cognitive gene enhancements and the capitalist meritocracy.

Abstract.AbstractText: The relationship between cognitive enhancements (CE) and human autonomy or authenticity is generally positioned as how CE impact human autonomy or authenticity. But rarely, if ever, do we consider whether the value and pursuit of CE is an authentic one. In this paper, I will argue that the moral permissibility of cognitive gene enhancements is undone by the legitimate concern that the near universal value for such modifications is likely driven by oppressive norms for superintelligence and productivity. I argue that these norms derive from the capitalist meritocracy: an economic system that structures inclusion and success based on patriarchal and racist norms of intelligence and productivity. The claim that the use of such enhancements fits within the autonomous scope of individual power is thus far weaker than it claims to be, particularly within the context of genetic modification.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Centre for Biomedical Science, Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, National University of Singapore, Singapore, Singapore. sprince@nus.edu.sg.

Grant.Agency: Social Science Research Council (Singapore) & Ministry of Education, Singapore

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Ethics approval and consent to participate: Not applicable. Consent for publication: Not applicable. Competing interests: The authors declare no competing interests.
